					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Spanish fashion designer (1895–1972)</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">In this <a href="/wiki/Spanish_name" class="mw-redirect" title="Spanish name">Spanish name</a>, the first or paternal <a href="/wiki/Surname" title="Surname">surname</a> is <i> Balenciaga</i> and the second or maternal family name is <i> Eizaguirre</i>.</div>

class="infobox-caption">Balenciaga in 1950</div></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Born</th><td class="infobox-data"><div style="display:inline" class="nickname">Cristóbal Balenciaga Eizaguirre</div><br><span style="display:none">(<span class="bday">1895-01-21</span>)</span>21 January 1895<br><div style="display:inline" class="birthplace"><a href="/wiki/Getaria,_Gipuzkoa" class="mw-redirect" title="Getaria, Gipuzkoa">Getaria</a>, Spain</div><span style="display:none" data-plural="1"></span></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Died</th><td class="infobox-data">23 March 1972<span style="display:none">(1972-03-23)</span> (aged 77)<br><div style="display:inline" class="deathplace"><a href="/wiki/X%C3%A0bia" title="Xàbia">Xàbia</a>, Spain</div><span style="display:none" data-plural="1"></span></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Resting place</th><td class="infobox-data label">Getaria Cemetery<span style="display:none" data-plural="0"></span></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Occupation</th><td class="infobox-data role">Fashion designer</td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Years active</th><td class="infobox-data">1917–1972</td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Known for</th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/Haute_couture" title="Haute couture">Haute couture</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Label</th><td class="infobox-data"><a href="/wiki/Balenciaga" title="Balenciaga">Balenciaga</a></td></tr><tr><th scope="row" class="infobox-label">Partner</th><td class="infobox-data">Władzio Jaworowski d'Attainville (–1948)</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><b>Cristóbal Balenciaga Eizaguirre</b> (<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1177148991">.mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%}</style><span class="IPA-label IPA-label-small">Spanish:</span> <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="es-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish" title="Help:IPA/Spanish">[kɾisˈtoβal<span class="wrap"> </span>βalenˈθjaɣa<span class="wrap"> </span>ejθaˈɣire]</a></span>; <a href="/wiki/Basque_language" title="Basque language">Basque</a>: <i lang="eu">Cristobal Balentziaga Eizagirre</i> <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="eu-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/Basque" title="Help:IPA/Basque">[kɾis̺toβal<span class="wrap"> </span>βalents̻i.aɣa<span class="wrap"> </span>eis̻aɣire]</a></span>; 21 January 1895 – 23 March 1972) was a Spanish fashion designer, and the founder of the <a href="/wiki/Balenciaga" title="Balenciaga">Balenciaga</a> clothing brand. He had a reputation as a couturier of uncompromising standards and was referred to as "the master of us all" by <a href="/wiki/Christian_Dior" title="Christian Dior">Christian Dior</a> and as "the only <i><a href="/wiki/Haute_couture" title="Haute couture">couturier</a></i> in the truest sense of the word" by <a href="/wiki/Coco_Chanel" title="Coco Chanel">Coco Chanel</a>, who continued, "The others are simply <a href="/wiki/Fashion_design" title="Fashion design">fashion designers</a>". On the day of his death, in 1972, <i><a href="/wiki/Women%27s_Wear_Daily" title="Women's Wear Daily">Women's Wear Daily</a></i> ran the headline "<a href="/wiki/The_king_is_dead,_long_live_the_king!" title="The king is dead, long live the king!">The King is Dead</a>".
</p><p>Since 2011 the purpose-built <a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum (page does not exist)">Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum</a><span class="noprint" style="font-size:85%; font-style: normal;"> [<a href="https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Museo_Balenciaga" class="extiw" title="es:Museo Balenciaga">es</a>]</span> has exhibited examples of his work in his birth town <a href="/wiki/Getaria,_Spain" title="Getaria, Spain">Getaria</a>. Many of the 1,200 pieces in the collection were supplied by his pupil <a href="/wiki/Hubert_de_Givenchy" title="Hubert de Givenchy">Hubert de Givenchy</a> and clients such as  <a href="/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis" title="Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis">First Lady Jacqueline Kennedy.</a>
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Early_life">Early life</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: Early life"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Balenciaga was born in the <a href="/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community)" title="Basque Country (autonomous community)">Basque</a> town of <a href="/wiki/Getaria,_Spain" title="Getaria, Spain">Getaria</a> (<a href="/wiki/Gipuzkoa" title="Gipuzkoa">Gipuzkoa</a>), Spain on 21 January 1895.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His father, José Balenciaga Basurto, was a mariner who died when Cristóbal was a boy, and his mother, Martina Eizaguirre Embil, a seamstress.<sup id="cite_ref-BT_2-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BT-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> As a child Balenciaga, together with his two siblings Agustina and Juan, often spent time with his mother as she worked.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-BT_2-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BT-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>  At the age of twelve, he began work as the apprentice of a tailor.<sup id="cite_ref-va_3-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-va-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> When he was a teenager, the <a href="/wiki/Marquess" title="Marquess">Marchioness</a> de Casa Torres, the foremost noblewoman in his town, became his customer and patron.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> She sent him to Madrid, where he was formally trained in tailoring.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Career">Career</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Career"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Balenciaga was successful during his early career as a designer in Spain.  He opened a boutique in <a href="/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n" title="San Sebastián">San Sebastián</a> in 1919,<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which expanded to include branches in Madrid and Barcelona.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The Spanish royal family and the aristocracy wore his designs, but when the <a href="/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War" title="Spanish Civil War">Spanish Civil War</a> forced him to close his stores, Balenciaga moved to Paris.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-va_3-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-va-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He opened his Paris couture house on <a href="/wiki/Avenue_George_V" title="Avenue George V">Avenue George V</a> in August 1937.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>However, it was not until the post-war years that the full scale of the inventiveness of his highly original designs became evident. In 1951, he totally transformed the silhouette, broadening the shoulders and removing the waist. In 1955, he designed the tunic dress, which later developed into the <a href="/wiki/Chemise" title="Chemise">chemise dress</a> of 1957. In 1959, his work culminated in the Empire line, with high-waisted dresses and coats cut like kimono.<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1960 he made the wedding dress for <a href="/wiki/Fabiola_of_Belgium" title="Fabiola of Belgium">Fabiola de Mora y Aragón</a> when <a href="/wiki/Wedding_of_Baudouin_of_Belgium_and_Fabiola_de_Mora_y_Arag%C3%B3n" title="Wedding of Baudouin of Belgium and Fabiola de Mora y Aragón">she married</a> King <a href="/wiki/Baudouin_of_Belgium" title="Baudouin of Belgium">Baudouin of Belgium</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He created many designs for socialite <a href="/wiki/Aline_Griffith,_Countess_of_Romanones" title="Aline Griffith, Countess of Romanones">Aline Griffith</a>, diplomat <a href="/wiki/Margarita_Salaverr%C3%ADa_Gal%C3%A1rraga" title="Margarita Salaverría Galárraga">Margarita Salaverría Galárraga</a>, and designer Meye Allende de Maier, considering them his muses.<sup id="cite_ref-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He taught fashion design classes, inspiring other designers including <a href="/wiki/Oscar_de_la_Renta" title="Oscar de la Renta">Oscar de la Renta</a>, <a href="/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Courr%C3%A8ges" title="André Courrèges">André Courrèges</a>, <a href="/wiki/Emanuel_Ungaro" title="Emanuel Ungaro">Emanuel Ungaro</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mila_Sch%C3%B6n" title="Mila Schön">Mila Schön</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Hubert_de_Givenchy" title="Hubert de Givenchy">Hubert de Givenchy</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-met_1-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-met-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His often spare, sculptural creations were considered masterworks of <i><a href="/wiki/Haute_couture" title="Haute couture">haute couture</a></i> in the 1950s and 1960s.
</p><p>His most famous clients were <a href="/wiki/Mona_von_Bismarck" title="Mona von Bismarck">Mona von Bismarck</a>, <a href="/wiki/Gloria_Guinness" title="Gloria Guinness">Gloria Guinness</a>, <a href="/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis" title="Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis">Jackie Kennedy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Grace_Kelly" title="Grace Kelly">Grace Kelly</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ava_Gardner" title="Ava Gardner">Ava Gardner</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn" title="Audrey Hepburn">Audrey Hepburn</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Retirement_and_death">Retirement and death</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Retirement and death"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Balenciaga retired from the high fashion world in 1968 at the age of 74 after working in Paris for thirty years. First, he decided to retire from active life in May 1968 and, a few months later, on 1 July, the newspapers broke the news of his headquarters in Paris and his studios in Madrid closing. The closing down of his business came by surprise even to his employees.<sup id="cite_ref-:0_9-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He justified the closing with his habitual clinical brevity: "High fashion is mortally wounded".<sup id="cite_ref-:0_9-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> On one hand, the intense imposing burden of the French taxes blew through the largest part of the profits and, on the other, Americans stopped buying French high fashion, largely because of General <a href="/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle" title="Charles de Gaulle">de Gaulle</a>'s anti-American politics. Americans had succeeded in representing close to seventy percent of the Balenciaga clientele.
</p><p>Balenciaga returned to Spain. He ended his days living in his house in <a href="/wiki/Altea" title="Altea">Altea</a> (<a href="/wiki/Province_of_Alicante" title="Province of Alicante">Alicante</a>), where he enjoyed painting, conversing, and eating in the company of some of his Spanish assistants.<sup id="cite_ref-:0_9-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>As an exception, four years after his retirement, he accepted the job of designing a gown for the wedding of <a href="/wiki/Carmen_Mart%C3%ADnez-Bordi%C3%BA" title="Carmen Martínez-Bordiú">Carmen Martínez-Bordiú</a>, the daughter of one of his most important clients, <a href="/wiki/Carmen_Franco,_1st_Duchess_of_Franco" title="Carmen Franco, 1st Duchess of Franco">Carmen Franco y Polo</a>, and hence, the granddaughter of dictator <a href="/wiki/Francisco_Franco" title="Francisco Franco">Francisco Franco</a>. Her wedding to <a href="/wiki/Alfonso,_Duke_of_Anjou_and_C%C3%A1diz" title="Alfonso, Duke of Anjou and Cádiz">Alfonso de Borbón y Dampierre</a>, a grandson of the Spanish king-in-exile, <a href="/wiki/Alfonso_XIII" title="Alfonso XIII">Alfonso XIII</a>, was celebrated on 8 March 1972, Martínez-Bordiu's dress being Balenciaga's last work.
</p><p>That same month of March 1972, Balenciaga went to spend a vacation at the <a href="/wiki/Paradores" title="Paradores">Parador</a> of <a href="/wiki/X%C3%A0bia" title="Xàbia">Jávea</a> (Alicante). He was not sick, nor known of any serious ailment, but unexpectedly on 23 March 1972, he suffered a heart attack, followed by heart failure. He was 77 years old at the time of his death.<sup id="cite_ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He was buried in the Getaria Cemetery.<sup id="cite_ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Personal_life">Personal life</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Personal life"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Balenciaga was gay,<sup id="cite_ref-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> although he kept his sexuality private throughout his life.<sup id="cite_ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The love of his life and his long time partner was Franco-Polish millionaire Władzio Jaworowski d'Attainville, who had helped set him up and fund him. When d'Attainville died in 1948, Balenciaga was so broken he considered closing the business.<sup id="cite_ref-15" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-15"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> His next collection after d'Attainville's death was designed entirely in black to mourn his loss.<sup id="cite_ref-16" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-16"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Work">Work</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=5" title="Edit section: Work"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>During the 1950s, designers like <a href="/wiki/Christian_Dior" title="Christian Dior">Christian Dior</a>, <a href="/wiki/Pierre_Balmain" title="Pierre Balmain">Pierre Balmain</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Coco_Chanel" title="Coco Chanel">Coco Chanel</a> emerged, creating pieces very representative to their fashion houses and to their own styles. An important protagonist for this period was Cristóbal Balenciaga. This Spanish fashion designer was known as "The King of Fashion" and "The Master of Us All" and was one of the great masterminds of the period. Balenciaga was born and raised in Spain, where he worked for the <a href="/wiki/List_of_heads_of_state_of_Spain" title="List of heads of state of Spain">Spanish royalty</a>, but because of the Spanish Civil War he moved to Paris where his genius was finally recognized, leading to a domination of the fashion scene.<sup id="cite_ref-17" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-17"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The most eye-catching designer of this period was Balenciaga because of his structural designs, which had never before been seen in the fashion world. He was a master of construction, and created all of his work without any sketches or illustrations beforehand. This was practically unheard of in his time, and still is to this day. In the age of couture, most all couturiers were mainly proficient in illustration, lacking profound knowledge of the craft. It is said that when designing his garments, he began by feeling the fabric, and only then could he form a design around what would best suit it. Compared to his counterparts, he obtained an edge with his deep understanding and unrelenting precision.<sup id="cite_ref-18" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-18"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> "He reshaped women's silhouette in the 1950s, so that clothes we think as typical of that decade are mostly dilutions of his work." (Irvine, 2013)<sup id="cite_ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Compared to some work like the <a href="/wiki/Dior#"New_Look"" title="Dior">New Look</a> from <a href="/wiki/Christian_Dior" title="Christian Dior">Christian Dior</a>, which featured full skirts and a tiny waist,<sup id="cite_ref-20" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-20"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Balenciaga worked to achieve what was seemingly the opposite. Much of Cristobal's work of the mid to later 50s showcased wildly avant garde designs which did not conform to the female body in the same ways; designs like the Sack Dress, which was developed alongside Hubert de Givenchy, the Cocoon Coat, and Babydoll Dress. He blurred the line of contemporary design by dropping waist lines and simplifying seams to achieve elegance in his designs. The one-seam Yoki coat's construction showcased this sewing approach; it featured a front and back that include the sleeves in their pattern pieces similar to the construction of a Kimono. He also invented multiple coats under his main line, Balenciaga, and the sister label, Eisa, which featured a back yoke that extended into the under sleeve, and also utilized a similar approach.
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Legacy">Legacy</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=6" title="Edit section: Legacy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>In the studio of Cristóbal Balenciaga, many couturiers formed, some of which would reach international fame and would get to have their own business. Among these include <a href="/wiki/Paco_Rabanne" title="Paco Rabanne">Paco Rabanne</a>, <a href="/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Courr%C3%A8ges" title="André Courrèges">André Courreges</a>, <a href="/wiki/Emanuel_Ungaro" title="Emanuel Ungaro">Emanuel Ungaro</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hubert_de_Givenchy" title="Hubert de Givenchy">Hubert de Givenchy</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Oscar_de_la_Renta" title="Oscar de la Renta">Óscar de la Renta</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-:0_9-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Balenciaga_brand">Balenciaga brand</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=7" title="Edit section: Balenciaga brand"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Balenciaga" title="Balenciaga">Balenciaga</a></div>
<p>The closing of Balenciaga's high fashion house in 1968 did not signify the extinction of "Balenciaga’s brand". The brand was inactive until 1986 when Jacques Konckier of Jacques Bogart bought the Balenciaga company.<sup id="cite_ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Although separate from its creator, Balenciaga has actively kept up in the fashion world since then as far as relevance. "His sense of proportion and measurement, and his vision and interpretation of the female body have anointed him as one of the more influential designers of all time".<sup id="cite_ref-23" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Balenciaga designs products of high fashion, ready-to-wear, perfumery, jewels, and other accessories. The Balenciaga business has mainly kept active in the perfumery and accessories spectrum. Popular items include the Motorcycle Bag<sup id="cite_ref-24" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>, as well as their shoes due too their originality. 
</p><p>Today the Balenciaga fashion house continues under the direction of Cedric Charbit and under the ownership of the <a href="/wiki/Kering" title="Kering">Kering</a> group.<sup id="cite_ref-25" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-25"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Presence_in_museums">Presence in museums</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=8" title="Edit section: Presence in museums"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Getaria_-_Palacio_de_Aldamar-Cristobal_Balenciaga_Museoa_2.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Getaria_-_Palacio_de_Aldamar-Cristobal_Balenciaga_Museoa_2.JPG/250px-Getaria_-_Palacio_de_Aldamar-Cristobal_Balenciaga_Museoa_2.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Getaria_-_Palacio_de_Aldamar-Cristobal_Balenciaga_Museoa_2.JPG/500px-Getaria_-_Palacio_de_Aldamar-Cristobal_Balenciaga_Museoa_2.JPG 1.5x" data-file-width="1280" data-file-height="960"></a><figcaption>Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum in Getaria</figcaption></figure>
<p>In Getaria, a foundation was established with the objective of creating a museum dedicated to Cristóbal Balenciaga. After numerous problems and the avoided judicial process about alleged inconsistencies in its management,<sup id="cite_ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> the <a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum (page does not exist)">Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum</a><span class="noprint" style="font-size:85%; font-style: normal;"> [<a href="https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Museo_Balenciaga" class="extiw" title="es:Museo Balenciaga">es</a>]</span> was inaugurated by <a href="/wiki/Queen_Sof%C3%ADa_of_Spain" title="Queen Sofía of Spain">Queen Sofía</a> on 7 June 2011, with the presence of Hubert de Givenchy, who was the honorific president of the foundation.<sup id="cite_ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The museum opened on 10 June 2011.<sup id="cite_ref-28" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-28"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> It has a collection of more than 1,200 pieces designed by him, some of them donations by disciples, like Givenchy, or clients, like Queen Fabiola – who donated her wedding dress to the foundation – and the heirs of Grace Kelly, although only one part displays simultaneously.<sup id="cite_ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-31" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The pieces are displayed on invisible mannequins.
</p><p>The historic collection of the <a href="/wiki/Museu_T%C3%A8xtil_i_d%27Indument%C3%A0ria" title="Museu Tèxtil i d'Indumentària">Museum of Textile and Clothing</a>, that is part of the <a href="/wiki/Design_Museum_of_Barcelona" title="Design Museum of Barcelona">Design Museum of Barcelona</a>, has an extensive number of dresses and accessories designed by Cristóbal Balenciaga, where its hats and headdresses stand out, for their great variety of shapes. The <a href="/wiki/Museo_del_Traje" title="Museo del Traje">Museum of Garment</a> (Madrid)<sup id="cite_ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> also has a collection of dresses designed by him. Within these exhibitions and pieces, people can find, like seen in the <a href="/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum" title="Victoria and Albert Museum">Victoria and Albert Museum</a> (London), "sculptural silhouettes that the forms of architecture drink from, in the geometric lines of their patterns and also in the set with the volumes that allowed to go without saying as far as unthinkable in 1958, with his babydoll model: inspired in children clothing, the dress left in flotation without pressure to the figure and with total freedom of movement to the user".<sup id="cite_ref-33" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>His work has been an object of numerous museum study expositions, starting with an anthology exposition that the <a href="/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art" title="Metropolitan Museum of Art">Metropolitan Museum of Art</a> (New York) provided in 1973, "The World of Balenciaga", which exhibited for six months, with 180 models on display. Balenciaga's garments accompanied the paintings of <a href="/wiki/Francisco_Goya" title="Francisco Goya">Goya</a>, <a href="/wiki/Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez" title="Diego Velázquez">Velázquez</a>, <a href="/wiki/El_Greco" title="El Greco">El Greco</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ignacio_Zuloaga" title="Ignacio Zuloaga">Zuloaga</a>, <a href="/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3" title="Joan Miró">Miró</a> or <a href="/wiki/Pablo_Picasso" title="Pablo Picasso">Picasso</a>, in which they had inspired the designs of the Basque couturier. It was the first time that an art museum of the importance of the Metropolitan added dresses to the category of works of art. In Spain, a namesake exposition was also celebrated, which was installed in the noble living rooms of the Palace of Library and National Museums in Madrid.
</p><p>On 24 March 2011, <a href="/wiki/De_Young_(museum)" class="mw-redirect" title="De Young (museum)">M. H. de Young Museum</a> (San Francisco) celebrated the opening of "Balenciaga and Spain", a 120-piece fashion retrospective of his career. "You can't even measure it", said <a href="/wiki/Rodarte" title="Rodarte">Rodarte</a> designer Laura Mulleavy, of Balenciaga's influence. The $2,500-a-ticket fund-raiser for the museum drew 350 guests, including <a href="/wiki/Marissa_Mayer" title="Marissa Mayer">Marissa Mayer</a>, <a href="/wiki/Jamie_Tisch" title="Jamie Tisch">Jamie Tisch</a>, <a href="/wiki/Gwyneth_Paltrow" title="Gwyneth Paltrow">Gwyneth Paltrow</a>, <a href="/wiki/Orlando_Bloom" title="Orlando Bloom">Orlando Bloom</a>, <a href="/wiki/Balthazar_Getty" title="Balthazar Getty">Balthazar Getty</a>, <a href="/wiki/Maggie_Rizer" title="Maggie Rizer">Maggie Rizer</a>, <a href="/wiki/Connie_Nielsen" title="Connie Nielsen">Connie Nielsen</a>, <a href="/wiki/Maria_Bello" title="Maria Bello">Maria Bello</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Mia_Wasikowska" title="Mia Wasikowska">Mia Wasikowska</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-34" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-34"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Queen_Letizia_of_Spain" title="Queen Letizia of Spain">Queen Letizia of Spain</a> wears a 1948 black dress and a 1962 fuchsia cape, designed by Balenciaga and owned by the Antoni de Montpalau Foundation, in the portrait made in 2024 by <a href="/wiki/Annie_Leibovitz" title="Annie Leibovitz">Annie Leibovitz</a> for the <a href="/wiki/Bank_of_Spain" title="Bank of Spain">Bank of Spain</a> portrait gallery.<sup id="cite_ref-ElMundo_35-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ElMundo-35"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Cultural_depictions">Cultural depictions</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga&action=edit&section=9" title="Edit section: Cultural depictions"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>His friend, the sculptor <a href="/wiki/Eduardo_Chillida" title="Eduardo Chillida">Eduardo Chillida</a>, made a sculpture-tribute called <i>Homage to Balenciaga</i>. San Sebastián, also pays homage with a promenade, that is situated in the neighborhood of <a href="/wiki/Igeldo" title="Igeldo">Igeldo</a>, where he had a house.
</p><p><i><a href="/wiki/Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga_(TV_series)" title="Cristóbal Balenciaga (TV series)">Cristóbal Balenciaga</a></i> is a <a href="/wiki/Disney%2B" title="Disney+">Disney+</a> biographical drama series starring <a href="/wiki/Alberto_San_Juan" title="Alberto San Juan">Alberto San Juan</a> in the titular role.<sup id="cite_ref-ElEspañol_36-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ElEspañol-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Nuno_Lopes" title="Nuno Lopes">Nuno Lopes</a> plays Cristóbal Balenciaga in the <a href="/wiki/Apple_TV%2B" class="mw-redirect" title="Apple TV+">Apple TV+</a> series about <a href="/wiki/Christian_Dior" title="Christian Dior">Christian Dior</a>, <i><a href="/wiki/The_New_Look_(TV_series)" title="The New Look (TV series)">The New Look</a></i>.<sup id="cite_ref-Fashion_Network_37-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fashion_Network-37"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Javier_Rey" title="Javier Rey">Javier Rey</a> plays Cristóbal Balenciaga in the <a href="/wiki/Telecinco" title="Telecinco">Telecinco</a> drama miniseries <i><a href="/wiki/Lo_que_escond%C3%ADan_sus_ojos" title="Lo que escondían sus ojos">Lo que escondían sus ojos</a></i>.<sup id="cite_ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>

<div class="rt-overlay"></div><div id="mw-teleport-target" class="vector-body"></div><a accesskey="v" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga?action=edit" class="oo-ui-element-hidden"></a></body></html>
markdown:
**Cristóbal Balenciaga Eizaguirre** (Spanish: [[kɾisˈtoβal βalenˈθjaɣa ejθaˈɣire]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"); [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language"): *Cristobal Balentziaga Eizagirre* [[kɾis̺toβal βalents̻i.aɣa eis̻aɣire]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Basque "Help:IPA/Basque"); 21 January 1895 – 23 March 1972) was a Spanish fashion designer, and the founder of the [Balenciaga](/wiki/Balenciaga "Balenciaga") clothing brand. He had a reputation as a couturier of uncompromising standards and was referred to as "the master of us all" by [Christian Dior](/wiki/Christian_Dior "Christian Dior") and as "the only *[couturier](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture")* in the truest sense of the word" by [Coco Chanel](/wiki/Coco_Chanel "Coco Chanel"), who continued, "The others are simply [fashion designers](/wiki/Fashion_design "Fashion design")". On the day of his death, in 1972, *[Women's Wear Daily](/wiki/Women%27s_Wear_Daily "Women's Wear Daily")* ran the headline "[The King is Dead](/wiki/The_king_is_dead,_long_live_the_king! "The king is dead, long live the king!")".

Since 2011 the purpose-built [Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum](/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Museo_Balenciaga "es:Museo Balenciaga")] has exhibited examples of his work in his birth town [Getaria](/wiki/Getaria,_Spain "Getaria, Spain"). Many of the 1,200 pieces in the collection were supplied by his pupil [Hubert de Givenchy](/wiki/Hubert_de_Givenchy "Hubert de Givenchy") and clients such as [First Lady Jacqueline Kennedy.](/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis "Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis")

## Early life

Balenciaga was born in the [Basque](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") town of [Getaria](/wiki/Getaria,_Spain "Getaria, Spain") ([Gipuzkoa](/wiki/Gipuzkoa "Gipuzkoa")), Spain on 21 January 1895.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1) His father, José Balenciaga Basurto, was a mariner who died when Cristóbal was a boy, and his mother, Martina Eizaguirre Embil, a seamstress.[[2]](#cite_note-BT-2) As a child Balenciaga, together with his two siblings Agustina and Juan, often spent time with his mother as she worked.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1)[[2]](#cite_note-BT-2) At the age of twelve, he began work as the apprentice of a tailor.[[3]](#cite_note-va-3) When he was a teenager, the [Marchioness](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess") de Casa Torres, the foremost noblewoman in his town, became his customer and patron.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1) She sent him to Madrid, where he was formally trained in tailoring.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1)

## Career

Balenciaga was successful during his early career as a designer in Spain. He opened a boutique in [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián") in 1919,[[4]](#cite_note-4) which expanded to include branches in Madrid and Barcelona.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1) The Spanish royal family and the aristocracy wore his designs, but when the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") forced him to close his stores, Balenciaga moved to Paris.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1)[[3]](#cite_note-va-3) He opened his Paris couture house on [Avenue George V](/wiki/Avenue_George_V "Avenue George V") in August 1937.[[1]](#cite_note-met-1)

However, it was not until the post-war years that the full scale of the inventiveness of his highly original designs became evident. In 1951, he totally transformed the silhouette, broadening the shoulders and removing the waist. In 1955, he designed the tunic dress, which later developed into the [chemise dress](/wiki/Chemise "Chemise") of 1957. In 1959, his work culminated in the Empire line, with high-waisted dresses and coats cut like kimono.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1960 he made the wedding dress for [Fabiola de Mora y Aragón](/wiki/Fabiola_of_Belgium "Fabiola of Belgium") when [she married](/wiki/Wedding_of_Baudouin_of_Belgium_and_Fabiola_de_Mora_y_Arag%C3%B3n "Wedding of Baudouin of Belgium and Fabiola de Mora y Aragón") King [Baudouin of Belgium](/wiki/Baudouin_of_Belgium "Baudouin of Belgium").[[6]](#cite_note-6) He created many designs for socialite [Aline Griffith](/wiki/Aline_Griffith,_Countess_of_Romanones "Aline Griffith, Countess of Romanones"), diplomat [Margarita Salaverría Galárraga](/wiki/Margarita_Salaverr%C3%ADa_Gal%C3%A1rraga "Margarita Salaverría Galárraga"), and designer Meye Allende de Maier, considering them his muses.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) He taught fashion design classes, inspiring other designers including [Oscar de la Renta](/wiki/Oscar_de_la_Renta "Oscar de la Renta"), [André Courrèges](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Courr%C3%A8ges "André Courrèges"), [Emanuel Ungaro](/wiki/Emanuel_Ungaro "Emanuel Ungaro"), [Mila Schön](/wiki/Mila_Sch%C3%B6n "Mila Schön"), and [Hubert de Givenchy](/wiki/Hubert_de_Givenchy "Hubert de Givenchy").[[1]](#cite_note-met-1) His often spare, sculptural creations were considered masterworks of *[haute couture](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture")* in the 1950s and 1960s.

His most famous clients were [Mona von Bismarck](/wiki/Mona_von_Bismarck "Mona von Bismarck"), [Gloria Guinness](/wiki/Gloria_Guinness "Gloria Guinness"), [Jackie Kennedy](/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis "Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis"), [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly"), [Ava Gardner](/wiki/Ava_Gardner "Ava Gardner"), and [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn").

## Retirement and death

Balenciaga retired from the high fashion world in 1968 at the age of 74 after working in Paris for thirty years. First, he decided to retire from active life in May 1968 and, a few months later, on 1 July, the newspapers broke the news of his headquarters in Paris and his studios in Madrid closing. The closing down of his business came by surprise even to his employees.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) He justified the closing with his habitual clinical brevity: "High fashion is mortally wounded".[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) On one hand, the intense imposing burden of the French taxes blew through the largest part of the profits and, on the other, Americans stopped buying French high fashion, largely because of General [de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle")'s anti-American politics. Americans had succeeded in representing close to seventy percent of the Balenciaga clientele.

Balenciaga returned to Spain. He ended his days living in his house in [Altea](/wiki/Altea "Altea") ([Alicante](/wiki/Province_of_Alicante "Province of Alicante")), where he enjoyed painting, conversing, and eating in the company of some of his Spanish assistants.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

As an exception, four years after his retirement, he accepted the job of designing a gown for the wedding of [Carmen Martínez-Bordiú](/wiki/Carmen_Mart%C3%ADnez-Bordi%C3%BA "Carmen Martínez-Bordiú"), the daughter of one of his most important clients, [Carmen Franco y Polo](/wiki/Carmen_Franco,_1st_Duchess_of_Franco "Carmen Franco, 1st Duchess of Franco"), and hence, the granddaughter of dictator [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"). Her wedding to [Alfonso de Borbón y Dampierre](/wiki/Alfonso,_Duke_of_Anjou_and_C%C3%A1diz "Alfonso, Duke of Anjou and Cádiz"), a grandson of the Spanish king-in-exile, [Alfonso XIII](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII "Alfonso XIII"), was celebrated on 8 March 1972, Martínez-Bordiu's dress being Balenciaga's last work.

That same month of March 1972, Balenciaga went to spend a vacation at the [Parador](/wiki/Paradores "Paradores") of [Jávea](/wiki/X%C3%A0bia "Xàbia") (Alicante). He was not sick, nor known of any serious ailment, but unexpectedly on 23 March 1972, he suffered a heart attack, followed by heart failure. He was 77 years old at the time of his death.[[10]](#cite_note-10) He was buried in the Getaria Cemetery.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Personal life

Balenciaga was gay,[[13]](#cite_note-13) although he kept his sexuality private throughout his life.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The love of his life and his long time partner was Franco-Polish millionaire Władzio Jaworowski d'Attainville, who had helped set him up and fund him. When d'Attainville died in 1948, Balenciaga was so broken he considered closing the business.[[15]](#cite_note-15) His next collection after d'Attainville's death was designed entirely in black to mourn his loss.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Work

During the 1950s, designers like [Christian Dior](/wiki/Christian_Dior "Christian Dior"), [Pierre Balmain](/wiki/Pierre_Balmain "Pierre Balmain"), and [Coco Chanel](/wiki/Coco_Chanel "Coco Chanel") emerged, creating pieces very representative to their fashion houses and to their own styles. An important protagonist for this period was Cristóbal Balenciaga. This Spanish fashion designer was known as "The King of Fashion" and "The Master of Us All" and was one of the great masterminds of the period. Balenciaga was born and raised in Spain, where he worked for the [Spanish royalty](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_state_of_Spain "List of heads of state of Spain"), but because of the Spanish Civil War he moved to Paris where his genius was finally recognized, leading to a domination of the fashion scene.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The most eye-catching designer of this period was Balenciaga because of his structural designs, which had never before been seen in the fashion world. He was a master of construction, and created all of his work without any sketches or illustrations beforehand. This was practically unheard of in his time, and still is to this day. In the age of couture, most all couturiers were mainly proficient in illustration, lacking profound knowledge of the craft. It is said that when designing his garments, he began by feeling the fabric, and only then could he form a design around what would best suit it. Compared to his counterparts, he obtained an edge with his deep understanding and unrelenting precision.[[18]](#cite_note-18) "He reshaped women's silhouette in the 1950s, so that clothes we think as typical of that decade are mostly dilutions of his work." (Irvine, 2013)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Compared to some work like the [New Look](/wiki/Dior#"New_Look" "Dior") from [Christian Dior](/wiki/Christian_Dior "Christian Dior"), which featured full skirts and a tiny waist,[[20]](#cite_note-20) Balenciaga worked to achieve what was seemingly the opposite. Much of Cristobal's work of the mid to later 50s showcased wildly avant garde designs which did not conform to the female body in the same ways; designs like the Sack Dress, which was developed alongside Hubert de Givenchy, the Cocoon Coat, and Babydoll Dress. He blurred the line of contemporary design by dropping waist lines and simplifying seams to achieve elegance in his designs. The one-seam Yoki coat's construction showcased this sewing approach; it featured a front and back that include the sleeves in their pattern pieces similar to the construction of a Kimono. He also invented multiple coats under his main line, Balenciaga, and the sister label, Eisa, which featured a back yoke that extended into the under sleeve, and also utilized a similar approach.

## Legacy

In the studio of Cristóbal Balenciaga, many couturiers formed, some of which would reach international fame and would get to have their own business. Among these include [Paco Rabanne](/wiki/Paco_Rabanne "Paco Rabanne"), [André Courreges](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Courr%C3%A8ges "André Courrèges"), [Emanuel Ungaro](/wiki/Emanuel_Ungaro "Emanuel Ungaro"), [Hubert de Givenchy](/wiki/Hubert_de_Givenchy "Hubert de Givenchy"), or [Óscar de la Renta](/wiki/Oscar_de_la_Renta "Oscar de la Renta").[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

### Balenciaga brand

The closing of Balenciaga's high fashion house in 1968 did not signify the extinction of "Balenciaga’s brand". The brand was inactive until 1986 when Jacques Konckier of Jacques Bogart bought the Balenciaga company.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Although separate from its creator, Balenciaga has actively kept up in the fashion world since then as far as relevance. "His sense of proportion and measurement, and his vision and interpretation of the female body have anointed him as one of the more influential designers of all time".[[23]](#cite_note-23) Balenciaga designs products of high fashion, ready-to-wear, perfumery, jewels, and other accessories. The Balenciaga business has mainly kept active in the perfumery and accessories spectrum. Popular items include the Motorcycle Bag[[24]](#cite_note-24), as well as their shoes due too their originality.

Today the Balenciaga fashion house continues under the direction of Cedric Charbit and under the ownership of the [Kering](/wiki/Kering "Kering") group.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Presence in museums

In Getaria, a foundation was established with the objective of creating a museum dedicated to Cristóbal Balenciaga. After numerous problems and the avoided judicial process about alleged inconsistencies in its management,[[26]](#cite_note-26) the [Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum](/w/index.php?title=Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Museo_Balenciaga "es:Museo Balenciaga")] was inaugurated by [Queen Sofía](/wiki/Queen_Sof%C3%ADa_of_Spain "Queen Sofía of Spain") on 7 June 2011, with the presence of Hubert de Givenchy, who was the honorific president of the foundation.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The museum opened on 10 June 2011.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) It has a collection of more than 1,200 pieces designed by him, some of them donations by disciples, like Givenchy, or clients, like Queen Fabiola – who donated her wedding dress to the foundation – and the heirs of Grace Kelly, although only one part displays simultaneously.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) The pieces are displayed on invisible mannequins.

The historic collection of the [Museum of Textile and Clothing](/wiki/Museu_T%C3%A8xtil_i_d%27Indument%C3%A0ria "Museu Tèxtil i d'Indumentària"), that is part of the [Design Museum of Barcelona](/wiki/Design_Museum_of_Barcelona "Design Museum of Barcelona"), has an extensive number of dresses and accessories designed by Cristóbal Balenciaga, where its hats and headdresses stand out, for their great variety of shapes. The [Museum of Garment](/wiki/Museo_del_Traje "Museo del Traje") (Madrid)[[32]](#cite_note-32) also has a collection of dresses designed by him. Within these exhibitions and pieces, people can find, like seen in the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum") (London), "sculptural silhouettes that the forms of architecture drink from, in the geometric lines of their patterns and also in the set with the volumes that allowed to go without saying as far as unthinkable in 1958, with his babydoll model: inspired in children clothing, the dress left in flotation without pressure to the figure and with total freedom of movement to the user".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

His work has been an object of numerous museum study expositions, starting with an anthology exposition that the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") (New York) provided in 1973, "The World of Balenciaga", which exhibited for six months, with 180 models on display. Balenciaga's garments accompanied the paintings of [Goya](/wiki/Francisco_Goya "Francisco Goya"), [Velázquez](/wiki/Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez "Diego Velázquez"), [El Greco](/wiki/El_Greco "El Greco"), [Zuloaga](/wiki/Ignacio_Zuloaga "Ignacio Zuloaga"), [Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró") or [Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso"), in which they had inspired the designs of the Basque couturier. It was the first time that an art museum of the importance of the Metropolitan added dresses to the category of works of art. In Spain, a namesake exposition was also celebrated, which was installed in the noble living rooms of the Palace of Library and National Museums in Madrid.

On 24 March 2011, [M. H. de Young Museum](/wiki/De_Young_(museum) "De Young (museum)") (San Francisco) celebrated the opening of "Balenciaga and Spain", a 120-piece fashion retrospective of his career. "You can't even measure it", said [Rodarte](/wiki/Rodarte "Rodarte") designer Laura Mulleavy, of Balenciaga's influence. The $2,500-a-ticket fund-raiser for the museum drew 350 guests, including [Marissa Mayer](/wiki/Marissa_Mayer "Marissa Mayer"), [Jamie Tisch](/wiki/Jamie_Tisch "Jamie Tisch"), [Gwyneth Paltrow](/wiki/Gwyneth_Paltrow "Gwyneth Paltrow"), [Orlando Bloom](/wiki/Orlando_Bloom "Orlando Bloom"), [Balthazar Getty](/wiki/Balthazar_Getty "Balthazar Getty"), [Maggie Rizer](/wiki/Maggie_Rizer "Maggie Rizer"), [Connie Nielsen](/wiki/Connie_Nielsen "Connie Nielsen"), [Maria Bello](/wiki/Maria_Bello "Maria Bello"), and [Mia Wasikowska](/wiki/Mia_Wasikowska "Mia Wasikowska").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The [Queen Letizia of Spain](/wiki/Queen_Letizia_of_Spain "Queen Letizia of Spain") wears a 1948 black dress and a 1962 fuchsia cape, designed by Balenciaga and owned by the Antoni de Montpalau Foundation, in the portrait made in 2024 by [Annie Leibovitz](/wiki/Annie_Leibovitz "Annie Leibovitz") for the [Bank of Spain](/wiki/Bank_of_Spain "Bank of Spain") portrait gallery.[[35]](#cite_note-ElMundo-35)

### Cultural depictions

His friend, the sculptor [Eduardo Chillida](/wiki/Eduardo_Chillida "Eduardo Chillida"), made a sculpture-tribute called *Homage to Balenciaga*. San Sebastián, also pays homage with a promenade, that is situated in the neighborhood of [Igeldo](/wiki/Igeldo "Igeldo"), where he had a house.

*[Cristóbal Balenciaga](/wiki/Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga_(TV_series) "Cristóbal Balenciaga (TV series)")* is a [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") biographical drama series starring [Alberto San Juan](/wiki/Alberto_San_Juan "Alberto San Juan") in the titular role.[[36]](#cite_note-ElEspañol-36) [Nuno Lopes](/wiki/Nuno_Lopes "Nuno Lopes") plays Cristóbal Balenciaga in the [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") series about [Christian Dior](/wiki/Christian_Dior "Christian Dior"), *[The New Look](/wiki/The_New_Look_(TV_series) "The New Look (TV series)")*.[[37]](#cite_note-Fashion_Network-37) [Javier Rey](/wiki/Javier_Rey "Javier Rey") plays Cristóbal Balenciaga in the [Telecinco](/wiki/Telecinco "Telecinco") drama miniseries *[Lo que escondían sus ojos](/wiki/Lo_que_escond%C3%ADan_sus_ojos "Lo que escondían sus ojos")*.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

new_content.transformed_content:
## Cristóbal Balenciaga: The Master of Us All

**Cristóbal Balenciaga Eizaguirre** (Spanish: [[kɾisˈtoβal βalenˈθjaɣa ejθaˈɣire]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"); Basque: Cristobal Balentziaga Eizagirre [[kɾis̺toβal βalents̻i.aɣa eis̻aɣire]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Basque "Help:IPA/Basque"); 21 January 1895 – 23 March 1972) was a Spanish fashion designer, and the founder of the [Balenciaga](/wiki/Balenciaga "Balenciaga") clothing brand. He had a reputation as a couturier of uncompromising standards and was referred to as 'the master of us all' by [Christian Dior](/wiki/Christian_Dior "Christian Dior") and as 'the only couturier in the truest sense of the word' by [Coco Chanel](/wiki/Coco_Chanel "Coco Chanel"), who continued, 'The others are simply fashion designers'.

### Early Life

Balenciaga was born in the Basque town of Getaria (Gipuzkoa), Spain on 21 January 1895. His father, José Balenciaga Basurto, was a mariner who died when Cristóbal was a boy, and his mother, Martina Eizaguirre Embil, a seamstress. As a child, Balenciaga, together with his two siblings Agustina and Juan, often spent time with his mother as she worked. At the age of twelve, he began work as the apprentice of a tailor. When he was a teenager, the Marchioness de Casa Torres, the foremost noblewoman in his town, became his customer and patron. She sent him to Madrid, where he was formally trained in tailoring.

### Career

Balenciaga was successful during his early career as a designer in Spain. He opened a boutique in San Sebastián in 1919, which expanded to include branches in Madrid and Barcelona. The Spanish royal family and the aristocracy wore his designs, but when the Spanish Civil War forced him to close his stores, Balenciaga moved to Paris. He opened his Paris couture house on Avenue George V in August 1937.

However, it was not until the post-war years that the full scale of the inventiveness of his highly original designs became evident. In 1951, he totally transformed the silhouette, broadening the shoulders and removing the waist. In 1955, he designed the tunic dress, which later developed into the chemise dress of 1957. In 1959, his work culminated in the Empire line, with high-waisted dresses and coats cut like kimono.

#### FAQ

**Q: What was Cristóbal Balenciaga known for?**
A: Cristóbal Balenciaga was known for his innovative and structural designs, which were ahead of their time. He was one of the most influential fashion designers of the 20th century.

**Q: Where did Balenciaga train as a tailor?**
A: Balenciaga trained as a tailor in Madrid, after being sent there by the Marchioness de Casa Torres.

**Q: When did Balenciaga open his first boutique?**
A: Balenciaga opened his first boutique in San Sebastián in 1919.

**Q: Why did Balenciaga move to Paris?**
A: Balenciaga moved to Paris during the Spanish Civil War, which forced him to close his stores in Spain.

**Q: What is the Balenciaga brand known for today?**
A: The Balenciaga brand is known for its high-fashion products, including ready-to-wear, perfumery, jewels, and other accessories.

### Legacy

In the studio of Cristóbal Balenciaga, many couturiers formed, some of which would reach international fame and would get to have their own business. Among these include Paco Rabanne, André Courreges, Emanuel Ungaro, Hubert de Givenchy, or Óscar de la Renta.

#### Balenciaga Brand

The closing of Balenciaga's high fashion house in 1968 did not signify the extinction of 'Balenciaga’s brand'. The brand was inactive until 1986 when Jacques Konckier of Jacques Bogart bought the Balenciaga company.

Although separate from its creator, Balenciaga has actively kept up in the fashion world since then as far as relevance. 'His sense of proportion and measurement, and his vision and interpretation of the female body have anointed him as one of the more influential designers of all time'. Balenciaga designs products of high fashion, ready-to-wear, perfumery, jewels, and other accessories. The Balenciaga business has mainly kept active in the perfumery and accessories spectrum. Popular items include the Motorcycle Bag, as well as their shoes due too their originality.

Today the Balenciaga fashion house continues under the direction of Cedric Charbit and under the ownership of the Kering group.

#### Presence in Museums

In Getaria, a foundation was established with the objective of creating a museum dedicated to Cristóbal Balenciaga. After numerous problems and the avoided judicial process about alleged inconsistencies in its management, the Cristóbal Balenciaga Museum was inaugurated by Queen Sofía on 7 June 2011, with the presence of Hubert de Givenchy, who was the honorific president of the foundation. The museum opened on 10 June 2011. It has a collection of more than 1,200 pieces designed by him, some of them donations by disciples, like Givenchy, or clients, like Queen Fabiola – who donated her wedding dress to the foundation – and the heirs of Grace Kelly, although only one part displays simultaneously.

The historic collection of the Museum of Textile and Clothing, that is part of the Design Museum of Barcelona, has an extensive number of dresses and accessories designed by Cristóbal Balenciaga, where its hats and headdresses stand out, for their great variety of shapes. The Museum of Garment (Madrid) also has a collection of dresses designed by him.

His work has been an object of numerous museum study expositions, starting with an anthology exposition that the Metropolitan Museum of Art (New York) provided in 1973, 'The World of Balenciaga', which exhibited for six months, with 180 models on display.

#### Cultural Depictions

His friend, the sculptor Eduardo Chillida, made a sculpture-tribute called Homage to Balenciaga. San Sebastián, also pays homage with a promenade, that is situated in the neighborhood of Igeldo, where he had a house.

*Cristóbal Balenciaga* is a Disney+ biographical drama series starring Alberto San Juan in the titular role. Nuno Lopes plays Cristóbal Balenciaga in the Apple TV+ series about Christian Dior, *The New Look*. Javier Rey plays Cristóbal Balenciaga in the Telecinco drama miniseries *Lo que escondían sus ojos*.
